summary: Cathy O'Neil wraps up her Lede Program for data journalism at Columbia, celebrating with students and her bluegrass band. Rather than writing a full post, she shares a curated reading list covering data visualization failures, private equity in distressed communities, payday loan targeting, income inequality, and gender bias in negotiations. The links reflect her ongoing interests in algorithmic targeting, economic justice, and data literacy.
key_points: The Lede Program taught data journalism students to recognize bad data visualization practices
Private equity firms buying distressed homes in places like Ferguson raises complex questions about investment and community impact
Payday loan companies use sophisticated online ad targeting that remains opaque
CEO-to-worker pay ratios in fast food (1252:1) illustrate extreme income inequality
Research shows people are more likely to lie to women in negotiations
money_quotes: My students all know better than this.
I'm actually not sure this is a bad thing, though, if nobody else is willing to do it. Please discuss.
I am still on the search for someone who knows exactly how those guys target their ads online.
The ratio of average CEO pay to average full-time worker pay is around 1252.
People lie to women in negotiations. I need to remember this.
key_insight: Even in a casual reading roundup, O'Neil's selections reveal her persistent focus on how money, power, and information asymmetries shape everyday life.
